text: Today we'll be viewing a video of a resection of a Meckel's diverticulum. In this particular case, the young lady presented with nonspecific abdominal pain in her lower pelvis. She previously had a cholecystectomy performed. While running a small bowel, she was found to have a large Meckel's diverticulum. This was in the typical position, approximately two feet away from the ileocecal valve.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic GIE stapler to transect the identified Meckel's diverticulum, ensuring the anterior lumen of the small bowel is adequately spaced to prevent obstruction.

The surgeon evaluates both the anterior and posterior aspects of the small bowel resection site. The posterior lumen is adjusted due to narrowing. A power GIA stapler is used to transect the bowel, and the lumen is checked for adequacy before firing the stapler, completing the transection without complications.

The surgeon inspects the staple line on the small bowel, confirming its integrity with no areas of concern. An endocatch bag is introduced into the surgical field, and the specimen is placed inside and removed smoothly.
